draft.introduction: Ezekiel 37 is the vision of the valley of dry bones. God commands the prophet to prophesy over the bones; they reassemble, receive sinews and flesh, and the breath of life enters them.

translation.text: The hand of the Lord was upon me, and he brought me out in the Spirit of the Lord and set me down in the middle of the valley; it was full of bones. And he said to me, “Son of man, can these bones live?” And I answered, “O Lord God, you know.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ χεὶρ κυρίου

translation.text: Then he said to me, “Prophesy to the breath; prophesy, son of man, and say to the breath, Thus says the Lord God: Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe upon these slain, that they may live.” So I prophesied as he commanded me, and the breath came into them, and they lived and stood on their feet, an exceedingly great army.

translator_notes.note: The Hebrew רוח (ruach) and Greek πνεῦμα both mean “wind,” “breath,” and “spirit” simultaneously. Ezekiel exploits all three senses: the wind comes, the corpses breathe, and the Spirit animates. Christian Pentecost theology draws directly on this triple meaning.
